content.text: The technical and the communicative tracks of incident response run in parallel. The technical track resolves the root cause; the communications track ensures that every affected audience -- executives, customers, support teams, and in serious cases regulators -- receives timely, accurate, and appropriately scoped information. A technically perfect resolution that is communicated poorly still damages customer trust and creates regulatory exposure.

The Comms Lead owns all outward-facing communication during the incident. The IC owns the facts and the approval gate; they do not draft external messages. This separation keeps the bridge channel focused on technical work while ensuring external messaging is consistent and reviewed before publication.

attrs.alt: Incident communications flow: War Room provides facts to Comms Lead, who drafts internal (exec, email) and external (status page, customer email) updates, all gated by IC approval before any external post. Five audience types shown at the bottom.

content.text: Different audiences need different information at different times. Sending the same update to all audiences creates noise for executives and insufficient detail for technical staff. The Comms Lead segments each update by audience before drafting.
Business impact, SLA risk, resolution ETA
Service impact, workaround if available, ETA
Within 60 min for P0/P1; discretionary P2
Impact summary, resolution status, no root cause detail
Incident description, data affected, remediation steps
Full post-incident analysis (optional for non-safety P0)
P0 safety or widespread data incident
Internal communications serve two purposes: keeping the incident team aligned, and giving leadership visibility so they can make business decisions without interrupting the IC.
The IC writes the executive briefing. It goes into #inc-{id}-exec and is also emailed to the VP Engineering distribution list within 15 minutes of declaration for P1 and within 5 minutes for P0.

The executive briefing must not contain log excerpts, stack traces, or hypothesis lists. If an executive asks for more technical detail, the IC redirects them to the bridge channel and offers a 5-minute call after the incident.

The Comms Lead sends a separate internal brief to the Support and Customer Success teams within 20 minutes of a customer-visible P2 or above. This brief is specifically formatted for support agents who are fielding customer inquiries.
External communications represent Anthropic publicly. All external messages must be reviewed and approved by the IC before publication. The Comms Lead drafts; the IC approves; no one else publishes.

Status page updates follow a three-state lifecycle: Investigating, Monitoring, and Resolved. Each state has a distinct message structure. The Comms Lead publishes the initial Investigating update within 60 minutes of a P0/P1 declaration.
What is affected + that investigation is underway; no root cause; no ETA
What happened (brief, no technical detail); mitigation in progress; ETA if known
Issue believed resolved; monitoring; will confirm stability before full resolution
Summary of impact, duration, confirmation of resolution

<!-- INVESTIGATING -->
**Investigating - Claude API Elevated Error Rate**
We are investigating reports of elevated error rates affecting the Claude API.
Affected services: API completions and streaming responses.
We will provide updates every 30 minutes.
Posted: {timestamp}

<!-- IDENTIFIED -->
**Identified - Claude API Elevated Error Rate**
We have identified the cause of the elevated error rates affecting the Claude API.
Our team is actively working on a fix. Affected services: API completions and streaming.
Estimated resolution: {time}.
Posted: {timestamp}

<!-- MONITORING -->
**Monitoring - Claude API Elevated Error Rate**
A fix has been deployed. We are monitoring to confirm full resolution.
If you continue to experience issues, please contact support.
Posted: {timestamp}

<!-- RESOLVED -->
**Resolved - Claude API Elevated Error Rate**
This incident has been resolved. Between {start} and {end}, some API requests
returned elevated error rates. All systems are now operating normally.
We will publish a full postmortem within 5 business days.
Posted: {timestamp}
What Not to Say in External Updates
Root cause before it is confirmed:
Saying "caused by a deployment" before it is verified forces a correction update if the diagnosis was wrong, compounding the trust damage.
Model versions, infrastructure vendor names, or internal service names provide no value to customers and may create competitive or legal exposure.
Do not name engineers, the IC, or any individual in external updates.
Stating how many requests were affected before the full scope is known can understate and require correction.
"This will never happen again" is unverifiable. Use "We are taking steps to prevent recurrence" instead.

Enterprise customers with SLA agreements receive direct communication in addition to the status page. The Comms Lead sends this within 30 minutes of a P2 or above that is customer-visible. The threshold is lower for enterprise because they have contractual expectations and dedicated support relationships.

import smtplib
from dataclasses import dataclass
from email.mime.text import MIMEText
from typing import List

@dataclass
class EnterpriseNotification:
    incident_id: str
    severity: str
    subject: str
    body: str
    recipients: List[str]

def send_enterprise_notification(notif: EnterpriseNotification) -> dict:
    """
    Send a direct enterprise customer notification.
    Returns {sent_count, failed_recipients}.
    """
    sent, failed = 0, []
    for recipient in notif.recipients:
        msg = MIMEText(notif.body, "plain")
        msg["Subject"] = notif.subject
        msg["From"] = "reliability@anthropic.com"
        msg["To"] = recipient
        try:
            with smtplib.SMTP("smtp.anthropic.com", 587) as s:
                s.starttls()
                s.login("reliability-bot", _get_smtp_password())
                s.sendmail(msg["From"], [recipient], msg.as_string())
            sent += 1
        except Exception as exc:
            failed.append({"recipient": recipient, "error": str(exc)})
    return {"sent_count": sent, "failed_recipients": failed}


def get_enterprise_recipients(incident_id: str, impact_scope: str) -> List[str]:
    """
    Query the customer database for enterprise accounts affected by this scope.
    impact_scope: 'all' | 'streaming' | 'function_calling' | 'us-east-1'
    """
    # In practice: query CRM or account DB filtered by active SLA tier
    # and whether their usage pattern matches impact_scope
    raise NotImplementedError("Connect to CRM API")
Data and safety incidents require additional communication steps beyond standard incident comms. These are not optional -- they are regulatory obligations and trust-critical actions.

When the Privacy team confirms that a P0 incident involves personal data belonging to EU residents, the 72-hour GDPR notification clock starts. The IC bot tracks this clock and alerts the Privacy team 24 hours before the deadline.
Notify the relevant supervisory authority (DPA) of the breach. This notification goes through Legal, not the Comms Lead.
Nature of the breach, categories of data, approximate number of individuals affected, likely consequences, and measures taken.
If not all information is available within 72 hours:
File a preliminary notification and follow up as information becomes available. Do not delay the initial filing to gather more information.
If the breach is likely to result in high risk to individuals, the Privacy team determines whether individual notification is required.

For incidents involving model safety policy violations (GDSC-002 scenario type), external communications are more restricted than standard service incidents. The Comms Lead and IC collaborate with the Safety team before any external statement.
Do not confirm the specific nature of the safety violation publicly.
Acknowledge service disruption and investigation; safety specifics are handled by the Safety communications team.
Safety incident postmortems are reviewed by Legal and Safety before publication. The standard 5-business-day timeline does not apply.
Depending on jurisdiction and incident type, the Safety team coordinates with external bodies. This is separate from GDPR obligations.
The IC programme tracks comms performance across incidents to identify systemic gaps in the communications workflow.
Time to first status page update (P0/P1)
Comms Lead coaching; review approval gate latency
Review recipient list refresh cadence and template automation
Comms Lead briefed; bot reminder interval reduced
Review approval gate; require IC sign-off earlier in drafting
Legal escalation; Privacy team alert threshold reduced to 48h
Comms Lead review; update enterprise template based on feedback
The Comms Lead runs a parallel workflow to the IC throughout the incident. Understanding the sequencing prevents gaps where no one is watching the comms queue.
Join bridge channel; confirm Comms Lead role with IC
Draft support brief; post to #inc-{id}-comms for review
IC reviews support brief; Comms Lead sends to Support team
Draft executive briefing; ping IC for approval
IC approves; Comms Lead posts to exec channel and sends email
Identify affected enterprise accounts; draft customer email
IC approves customer email; Comms Lead sends to affected enterprises
Draft first status page update (Investigating state)
IC approves; Comms Lead publishes Investigating update

For P0 incidents, all timings compress: the support brief target is 5 minutes, the exec brief target is 10 minutes, and the status page update must publish within 30 minutes of declaration if the outage is total.
The IC bot enforces the approval gate for external communications. No one can publish a status page update or send a customer email through the bot without the IC running the approval command first.

The postmortem is the final external communication artefact for significant incidents. It is published on the Anthropic blog or status page for P0 incidents and made available to affected enterprise customers for P1 incidents within five business days of resolution.
Technical review complete: root cause confirmed and documented.
Legal review: for P0 data or safety incidents, Legal has cleared the publication.
Customer references removed: no customer names, usage patterns, or identifying details in the public version.
Action items confirmed: at least two specific, owner-assigned action items included. Vague items like 'improve monitoring' are blocked by the review.
Comms Lead reviews tone: postmortem is blameless, factual, and includes an apology for customer impact.
IC approves final version before publication.
Postmortem Summary Template for Enterprise Customers

Incident communications are segmented by audience: internal engineering gets full detail through the bridge, executives get business-impact briefings in the exec channel and via email, enterprise customers receive direct notifications within 30 minutes, and the public status page gets state-transition updates (Investigating, Identified, Monitoring, Resolved) with no unconfirmed root cause or internal detail. The Comms Lead drafts all external messages; the IC approves before any publication. P0 data incidents trigger the 72-hour GDPR Article 33 notification clock, tracked by the IC bot. Safety incident communications are additionally reviewed by the Safety team before external publication. Comms performance is tracked through six metrics reviewed monthly to surface template, approval-gate, and notification-timing gaps.
